title: Basis Sets Reference
description: Technical reference for quantum chemistry basis sets

sections.title: What are Basis Sets?
sections.content:
A **basis set** is a set of mathematical functions used to represent molecular orbitals. The choice of basis set determines:
- Accuracy of the calculation
- Computational cost (number of qubits)
- Types of chemistry that can be described

sections.title: Minimal Basis Sets
sections.content:
**STO-3G (Slater-Type Orbital, 3 Gaussians)**

The smallest practical basis set. Each atomic orbital is represented by 3 Gaussian functions fitted to a Slater-type orbital.

| Atom | Orbitals | Functions |
|------|----------|----------|
| H | 1s | 1 |
| C | 1s, 2s, 2px, 2py, 2pz | 5 |
| O | 1s, 2s, 2px, 2py, 2pz | 5 |

**Pros:**
- Smallest qubit count
- Fast calculations
- Good for qualitative trends

**Cons:**
- Poor bond lengths (typically too long)
- Inaccurate energies
- Missing polarization/correlation effects

**Use When:**
- Learning/testing VQE
- Initial exploration
- Very large systems

sections.title: Split-Valence Basis Sets
sections.content:
**6-31G Family**

Core orbitals: 6 Gaussians
Valence orbitals: 3 Gaussians (contracted) + 1 Gaussian (diffuse)

**Variants:**
- **6-31G**: Base split-valence
- **6-31G***: + polarization on heavy atoms (d functions)
- **6-31G**: + polarization on H (p functions)
- **6-31+G**: + diffuse functions on heavy atoms
- **6-31++G**: + diffuse functions on all atoms

| Basis | H functions | C functions |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| 6-31G | 2 | 9 |
| 6-31G* | 2 | 15 |
| 6-31G** | 5 | 15 |

**Use When:**
- Quantitative geometry optimization
- Reaction energies
- Standard computational chemistry

sections.title: Correlation-Consistent Basis Sets
sections.content:
**Dunning's cc-pVXZ Family**

Systematically designed for correlated calculations.

| Basis | Quality | H functions | C functions |
|-------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| cc-pVDZ | Double-zeta | 5 | 14 |
| cc-pVTZ | Triple-zeta | 14 | 30 |
| cc-pVQZ | Quadruple-zeta | 30 | 55 |

**aug-cc-pVXZ**: Add diffuse functions for anions, excited states

**Pros:**
- Systematic convergence to complete basis set limit
- Best for high-accuracy work
- Well-characterized errors

**Cons:**
- Large qubit counts
- Expensive for VQE

**Use When:**
- Benchmark calculations
- When accuracy is critical
- Extrapolation to CBS limit

sections.title: Qubit Requirements by Basis
sections.content:
**Qubits needed = 2 × (number of spatial orbitals)**

| Molecule | STO-3G | 6-31G | cc-pVDZ |
|----------|--------|-------|--------|
| H₂ | 4 | 8 | 20 |
| LiH | 12 | 22 | 46 |
| BeH₂ | 14 | 26 | 58 |
| H₂O | 14 | 26 | 48 |
| NH₃ | 16 | 30 | 58 |
| CH₄ | 18 | 34 | 68 |
| C₂H₄ | 28 | 52 | 116 |
| Benzene | 72 | 132 | 264 |

**Note:** Active space methods can reduce qubit counts significantly!

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.content:
**For VQE on NISQ Devices:**
1. Start with STO-3G for development
2. Use 6-31G for production results
3. Active space selection is crucial for larger systems

**For Accurate Benchmarks:**
1. Use cc-pVDZ minimum
2. Extrapolate using cc-pVDZ/cc-pVTZ
3. Compare with experimental data

**Active Space Selection:**
For large molecules, select important orbitals:
- HOMO-n to LUMO+m (frontier orbitals)
- Orbitals involved in bond breaking/forming
- Use CASSCF or similar to identify active space
